stimulus: In a recent paper on bulk heterojunction organic solar cells (a class of thin-film photovoltaic device that converts sunlight into electricity using blended polymer–fullerene films), Priya Venkatesan and colleagues describe a method for depositing the cell's photoactive layer using a slot-die coating system. Conventional photoactive layer deposition is performed by spin coating a polymer solution onto small substrates, a technique that can waste as much as 90% of the polymer solution and thus drive up production costs at industrial volumes—a constraint that, according to the researchers, slot-die coating, which they characterize as "continuous, scalable, and material-efficient," may largely eliminate.
stem: What does the text most strongly suggest about conventional photoactive layer deposition?

answerOptions.content: It generally wastes more polymer solution than other established deposition techniques do.
It is somewhat imprecise and therefore reduces the efficiency with which bulk heterojunction cells convert sunlight.
It is more expensive at industrial scale than deposition methods used for other solar cell architectures.
It may be less well suited to manufacturing bulk heterojunction organic solar cells at industrial volumes than an alternative method.

rationale:
Choice D is the best answer. The text says spin coating wastes much of the polymer solution and "drive[s] up production costs at industrial volumes," a constraint that slot-die coating—described as "continuous, scalable, and material-efficient"—may largely eliminate. This strongly implies that the conventional approach is comparatively less suited to large-scale manufacturing of these cells than the alternative slot-die method.

Choice A is incorrect because the text only contrasts spin coating with slot-die coating; it does not compare spin coating to multiple established deposition techniques. Choice B is incorrect because the text never claims the efficiency of the resulting cells depends on the deposition method. Choice C is incorrect because the text does not compare costs across solar cell architectures.
